([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *XXIes Jeux olympiques d'hiver*) and also known as **Vancouver 2010** ([Squamish](/wiki/Squamish_language "Squamish language"): *K'emk'emeláy̓ 2010*), were an international winter [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held from [February](/wiki/February "February") 12 to 28, 2010 in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), British Columbia, Canada, with some events held in the surrounding suburbs of [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond,_British_Columbia "Richmond, British Columbia"), [West Vancouver](/wiki/West_Vancouver "West Vancouver") and the [University of British Columbia](/wiki/University_of_British_Columbia "University of British Columbia"), and in the nearby [resort town](/wiki/Resort_town "Resort town") of [Whistler](/wiki/Whistler,_British_Columbia "Whistler, British Columbia"). It was regarded by the [Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") to be among the most successful Olympic games in history, in both attendance and coverage.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Approximately 2,600 athletes from 82 nations participated in 86 events in fifteen disciplines.[[2]](#cite_note-athletes_number-2) Both the [Winter Olympic](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics") and [Paralympic Games](/wiki/2010_Winter_Paralympics "2010 Winter Paralympics") were organized by the [Vancouver Organizing Committee](/wiki/Vancouver_Organizing_Committee_for_the_2010_Olympic_and_Paralympic_Winter_Games "Vancouver Organizing Committee for the 2010 Olympic and Paralympic Winter Games") (VANOC), headed by [John Furlong](/wiki/John_Furlong_(CEO) "John Furlong (CEO)"). The 2010 Winter Games were the third [Olympics](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") to be hosted by Canada, and the first to be held within the province of British Columbia. Canada had hosted the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), Quebec, and the [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") in [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), Alberta.

The **2010 Winter Olympics**, officially known as the **XXI Olympic Winter Games** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *XXIes Jeux olympiques d'hiver*) and also known as **Vancouver 2010** ([Squamish](/wiki/Squamish_language "Squamish language"): *K'emk'emeláy̓ 2010*), were an international winter [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held from [February](/wiki/February "February") 12 to 28, 2010 in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), British Columbia, Canada, with some events held in the surrounding suburbs of [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond,_British_Columbia "Richmond, British Columbia"), [West Vancouver](/wiki/West_Vancouver "West Vancouver") and the [University of British Columbia](/wiki/University_of_British_Columbia "University of British Columbia"), and in the nearby [resort town](/wiki/Resort_town "Resort town") of [Whistler](/wiki/Whistler,_British_Columbia "Whistler, British Columbia"). It was regarded by the [Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") to be among the most successful Olympic games in history, in both attendance and coverage.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Approximately 2,600 athletes from 82 nations participated in 86 events in fifteen disciplines.[[2]](#cite_note-athletes_number-2) Both the [Winter Olympic](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics") and [Paralympic Games](/wiki/2010_Winter_Paralympics "2010 Winter Paralympics") were organized by the [Vancouver Organizing Committee](/wiki/Vancouver_Organizing_Committee_for_the_2010_Olympic_and_Paralympic_Winter_Games "Vancouver Organizing Committee for the 2010 Olympic and Paralympic Winter Games") (VANOC), headed by [John Furlong](/wiki/John_Furlong_(CEO) "John Furlong (CEO)"). The 2010 Winter Games were the third [Olympics](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") to be hosted by Canada, and the first to be held within the province of British Columbia. Canada had hosted the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), Quebec, and the [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") in [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), Alberta.

Following Olympic tradition, incumbent Vancouver mayor [Sam Sullivan](/wiki/Sam_Sullivan "Sam Sullivan") received the [Olympic flag](/wiki/Olympic_symbols "Olympic symbols") during the [closing ceremony](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics_closing_ceremony "2006 Winter Olympics closing ceremony") of the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics") in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), Italy. The flag was raised in a special ceremony on February 28, 2006, and was on display at [Vancouver City Hall](/wiki/Vancouver_City_Hall "Vancouver City Hall") until the [Vancouver opening ceremony](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics_opening_ceremony "2010 Winter Olympics opening ceremony"). The event was officially opened by [Governor General](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") [Michaëlle Jean](/wiki/Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean "Michaëlle Jean"),[[3]](#cite_note-govgen-3) accompanied by the Canadian Prime Minister [Stephen Harper](/wiki/Stephen_Harper "Stephen Harper") and [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) president [Jacques Rogge](/wiki/Jacques_Rogge "Jacques Rogge").[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

Vancouver 2010 was immensely successful, with the official Olympic Committee press release citing "Full venues, record public attendance: Over 97% of the 1.54 million tickets available were sold, with 71% to the Canadian public at an average price of $139. Adding in international public ticket sales, the percentage of ticket sales to the public exceeds 75% of all available tickets."[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Vancouver 2010 also achieved record global television coverage as per the Olympic Committee, more than double the viewership of the preceding Turin 2006 games and triple the available coverage of [Salt Lake City 2002](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics"), and is "recognized in numerous post Games reports as being among the most widely viewed and well received Games in Olympic history, both in Canada and internationally."[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Vancouver 2010's [gold medal game](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament#Gold_medal_game "Ice hockey at the 2010 Winter Olympics – Men's tournament") between Canada and USA "was the most watched hockey game ever with viewership of 114 million viewers around the world."[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

For the first time, Canada won gold in an [official sport](/wiki/Olympic_sports "Olympic sports") as the host nation of the Olympics, having failed to do so in 1976 and 1988 (although Canada won the 1988 woman's curling event in Calgary when it was still only a [demonstration sport](/wiki/Demonstration_sport "Demonstration sport")).[[5]](#cite_note-5) Canada clinched their first gold medal on the second day of the competition, first topping the gold medal tally on the second-to-last day of competition, and went on to become the first host nation since [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1952_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1952 Winter Olympics") in [1952](/wiki/1952_Winter_Olympics "1952 Winter Olympics") to lead the gold medal count.[[6]](#cite_note-clinch-6) Canada broke the record for the most gold medals won at a single Winter Olympics (14), which had been set by the [Soviet Union in 1976](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1976_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1976 Winter Olympics") and [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 2002 Winter Olympics") in [2002](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics") (13).[[7]](#cite_note-RecordGolds-7) The [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 2010 Winter Olympics") won the most medals in total, marking their second time doing so at the Winter Olympics, and broke the record for the most medals won during the Winter Games (37), a record held until then by [Germany in 2002](/wiki/Germany_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 2002 Winter Olympics") (36).[[6]](#cite_note-clinch-6) Athletes from [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Slovakia at the 2010 Winter Olympics")[[8]](#cite_note-8) and [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Belarus at the 2010 Winter Olympics")[[9]](#cite_note-9) won the first Winter Olympic gold medals for their nations.

## Bid and preparations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Bid and preparations")]

Main article: [Bids for the 2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Bids_for_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Bids for the 2010 Winter Olympics")

2010 Winter Olympics bidding results

| City | Country | Round | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 2 |
| **[Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver")** | **[Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada")** | 40 | **56** |
| [Pyeongchang](/wiki/Pyeongchang "Pyeongchang") | [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") | **51** | 53 |
| [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg") | [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") | 16 | – |

When Vancouver was bidding for the games, it proposed different dates (5-21 February for the XXI Olympic Winter Games and 5–14 March for the X Paralympic Winter Games).[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

The concept of a bid by [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") to host the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") first appeared at the [1960 Games](/wiki/1960_Winter_Olympic_Games "1960 Winter Olympic Games") in [Squaw Valley](/wiki/Squaw_Valley_Ski_Resort "Squaw Valley Ski Resort"), where the Canadian representative of the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) Sidney Dawes discusses the possibility of hosting a Games in [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") if a venue was found near Vancouver.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVANOC2010a15-10) Shortly afterwards, the Garibaldi Olympic Development Association (GODA) was created in order to prepare a bid to host the Winter Games in the region of [Mount Garibaldi](/wiki/Mount_Garibaldi "Mount Garibaldi"), near [Whistler](/wiki/Whistler,_British_Columbia "Whistler, British Columbia"). In 1961, GODA considered developing a bid for the [1968 Winter Games](/wiki/1968_Winter_Olympic_Games "1968 Winter Olympic Games"), but the [Canadian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Canadian_Olympic_Committee "Canadian Olympic Committee") (COC) preferred the cities of [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary") and [Banff](/wiki/Banff,_Alberta "Banff, Alberta"), in [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), as the best opportunity for a Canadian bid to succeed.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVANOC2010a15-10) Calgary's candidacy ultimately failed, and the 1968 Games were awarded to [Grenoble](/wiki/Grenoble "Grenoble").[[11]](#cite_note-CgyHld19810930-11)

A development program for the Whistler and Mount Garibaldi region was then launched to host the [1972 Games](/wiki/1972_Olympic_Winter_Games "1972 Olympic Winter Games"). It included the development of road infrastructure, electrical network and drinking water, which was still absent in this region.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVANOC2010a16-12) But once again, Banff was chosen to represent Canada as the potential host city for the 1972 Games, which were eventually awarded to [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[[11]](#cite_note-CgyHld19810930-11) In 1968, the GODA was finally chosen by the Canadian Olympic Committee with the aim of making a joint candidacy with the city of [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") for the organization of the [1976 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1976_Winter_Olympics "1976 Winter Olympics"). However, the chances of Vancouver's candidacy dwindled when [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") was selected to host the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics"), and the Vancouver-Garibaldi candidacy was thus eliminated in the first round of voting.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVANOC2010a16-12) [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") was again a candidate for the [1980 Games](/wiki/1980_Olympic_Winter_Games "1980 Olympic Winter Games"), but withdrew at the last moment.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] For the [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics"), [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary") was chosen as the preferred site for the Canadian bid,[[13]](#cite_note-13) and would go on to win the election and thus become the first Canadian city to host the Winter Olympics.

For the 2010 Games, the Canadian Olympic Association chose Vancouver as the Canadian candidate city over Calgary, which sought to re-host the Games, and [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"), which had lost the 2002 Olympic bid in 1995. On the first round of voting on November 21, 1998, Vancouver-Whistler had 26 votes, Quebec City had 25 and Calgary had 21. On December 3, 1998, the second and final round of voting occurred between the two leading contenders, which saw Vancouver win with 40 votes compared to Quebec City's 32 votes. Vancouver had also bid for the [1976](/wiki/Bids_for_the_1976_Winter_Olympics "Bids for the 1976 Winter Olympics") games, which were first awarded to Denver, then to Innsbruck and the [1980](/wiki/1980_Winter_Olympics "1980 Winter Olympics") games, which were awarded to Lake Placid.

After the [bribery scandal](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympic_bid_scandal "2002 Winter Olympic bid scandal") over the candidacy of the [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City") bid for the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics") (which resulted in Quebec City asking for compensation (C$8 million) for its unsuccessful bid),[[14]](#cite_note-14) many of the rules of the bidding process were changed in 1999. The [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) created the Evaluation Commission, which was appointed on October 24, 2002. Prior to the bidding for the [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics"), host cities would often fly members of the IOC to their city where they toured the city and were provided with gifts. The lack of oversight and transparency often led to allegations of money for votes. Afterward, changes brought forth by the IOC bidding rules were tightened, and more focused on technical aspects of candidate cities. The team analyzed the candidate city features and provided its input back to the IOC.

Vancouver won the bid to host the Olympics by a vote of the International Olympic Committee on July 2, 2003, at the 115th IOC Session held in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), Czech Republic. The result was announced by IOC President [Jacques Rogge](/wiki/Jacques_Rogge "Jacques Rogge").[[15]](#cite_note-15) Vancouver faced two other finalists shortlisted that same February: [Pyeongchang](/wiki/Pyeongchang_County "Pyeongchang County"), South Korea, and [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg"), Austria. Pyeongchang, which later won the rights of hosting both [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics") and [2024 Winter Youth Olympics](/wiki/2024_Winter_Youth_Olympics "2024 Winter Youth Olympics") (as a part of the [Gangwon Province](/wiki/Gangwon_Province_(South_Korea) "Gangwon Province (South Korea)")) had the most votes of the three cities in the first round of voting, in which Salzburg was eliminated. In the run-off, all but two of the members who had voted for Salzburg voted for Vancouver. It was the closest vote by the IOC since Sydney, Australia beat Beijing for the 2000 Summer Olympics by two votes. Vancouver's victory came almost two years after Toronto's [2008 Summer Olympic bid](/wiki/Bids_for_the_2008_Summer_Olympics "Bids for the 2008 Summer Olympics") was defeated by Beijing in a landslide vote.

The Vancouver Olympic Committee (VANOC) spent C$16.6 million on upgrading facilities at [Cypress Mountain](/wiki/Cypress_Mountain "Cypress Mountain"), which hosted the freestyle (aerials, moguls, ski cross) and snowboarding events. With the opening in February 2009 of the C$40 million [Vancouver Olympic/Paralympic Centre](/wiki/Vancouver_Olympic/Paralympic_Centre "Vancouver Olympic/Paralympic Centre") at [Hillcrest Park](/wiki/Hillcrest_Park "Hillcrest Park"), which hosted curling, every sports venue for the 2010 Games was completed on time and at least one year prior to the Games.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Financials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Financials")]

### Operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Operations")]

In 2004, the operational cost of the 2010 Winter Olympics was estimated to be Canadian $1.354 billion (about [£](/wiki/%C2%A3 "£")828,499,787, [€](/wiki/%E2%82%AC "€")975,033,598 or [US$](/wiki/US$ "US$")1,314,307,896). As of mid-2009 it was projected to be C$1.76 billion,[[18]](#cite_note-huffingtonpost1-18) mostly raised from non-government sources,[[18]](#cite_note-huffingtonpost1-18) primarily through sponsorships and the auction of national broadcasting rights. C$580 million was the taxpayer-supported budget to construct or renovate venues throughout [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") and [Whistler](/wiki/Whistler,_British_Columbia "Whistler, British Columbia"). A final audit conducted by [PricewaterhouseCoopers](/wiki/PricewaterhouseCoopers "PricewaterhouseCoopers") released in December 2010 revealed total operation cost to have been $1.84 billion and came in on budget resulting in neither surplus nor deficit. Construction of venues also came on budget with a total cost of $603 million.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

PricewaterhouseCoopers' study estimated a total contribution to the BC economy of $2.3 billion of Gross Domestic Product, and as well creating 45,000 jobs and contributing an additional $463 million to the tourism industry while venue construction by [VANOC](/wiki/Vancouver_Organizing_Committee_for_the_2010_Olympic_and_Paralympic_Winter_Games "Vancouver Organizing Committee for the 2010 Olympic and Paralympic Winter Games") and 3rd parties added $1.22 billion to the economy, far short of the $10 billion forecast by Premier [Gordon Campbell](/wiki/Gordon_Campbell_(Canadian_politician) "Gordon Campbell (Canadian politician)"). The study also said that hosting the Olympics was one of many reasons why the provincial debt grew by $24 billion during the decade. Indirect Olympic Games costs (e.g. expanded rail network, highways, security, paid time off for government employees "volunteering" etc.) totalled in excess of 7 billion.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In 2011, the provincial auditor-general declined to conduct a post-Games audit.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Security costs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Security costs")]

C$200 million was expected to be spent for security, which was organized through a special body, the [Integrated Security Unit](/wiki/Integrated_Security_Unit "Integrated Security Unit"), of which the [Royal Canadian Mounted Police](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police") (RCMP) was the lead agency; other government agencies such as the [Vancouver Police Department](/wiki/Vancouver_Police_Department "Vancouver Police Department"), [Canada Border Services Agency](/wiki/Canada_Border_Services_Agency "Canada Border Services Agency"), [Canadian Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces"), and [police agencies across Canada](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Canada "Law enforcement in Canada"). The [Canadian Security Intelligence Service](/wiki/Canadian_Security_Intelligence_Service "Canadian Security Intelligence Service") (CSIS) also played a role. That number was later revealed to be in the region of C$1 billion, an amount in excess of five times what was originally estimated.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Venues")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Richmond_Olympic_Oval_front_view.jpg/250px-Richmond_Olympic_Oval_front_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Richmond_Olympic_Oval_front_view.jpg)

[Richmond Olympic Oval](/wiki/Richmond_Olympic_Oval "Richmond Olympic Oval"): speed skating long track venue

Main article: [Venues of the 2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Venues_of_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Venues of the 2010 Winter Olympics")

Some venues, including the [Richmond Olympic Oval](/wiki/Richmond_Olympic_Oval "Richmond Olympic Oval"), were at sea level, a rarity for the Winter Games.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The 2010 Games were also the first—Winter or Summer—to have had an Opening Ceremony held indoors.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Greater Vancouver was the most populous metropolitan area ever to hold the Winter Games.[[25]](#cite_note-25) In February, the month when the Games were held, Vancouver has an average temperature of 4.8 °C (40.6 °F).[[26]](#cite_note-26) The average temperature as measured at [Vancouver International Airport](/wiki/Vancouver_International_Airport "Vancouver International Airport") was 7.1 °C (44.8 °F) for the month of February 2010.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The opening and closing ceremonies were held at [BC Place Stadium](/wiki/BC_Place "BC Place"), which received over C$150 million in major renovations. Competition venues in Greater Vancouver included the [Pacific Coliseum](/wiki/Pacific_Coliseum "Pacific Coliseum"), the [Vancouver Olympic/Paralympic Centre](/wiki/Hillcrest_Centre "Hillcrest Centre"), the [UBC Winter Sports Centre](/wiki/UBC_Winter_Sports_Centre "UBC Winter Sports Centre"), the [Richmond Olympic Oval](/wiki/Richmond_Olympic_Oval "Richmond Olympic Oval") and [Cypress Mountain](/wiki/Cypress_Mountain "Cypress Mountain"). GM Place, now known as [Rogers Arena](/wiki/Rogers_Arena "Rogers Arena"), played host to [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") events, being renamed *Canada Hockey Place* for the duration of the Games since corporate sponsorship is not allowed for an Olympic venue.[[28]](#cite_note-chp-28) Renovations included the removal of advertising from the ice surface and conversion of some seating to accommodate the media.[[28]](#cite_note-chp-28) The 2010 Winter Olympics marked the first time an Olympic hockey game was played on a rink sized according to NHL rules instead of [international](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") specifications. Competition venues in Whistler included Whistler Creekside at the [Whistler Blackcomb](/wiki/Whistler_Blackcomb "Whistler Blackcomb") ski resort, the [Whistler Olympic Park](/wiki/Whistler_Olympic_Park "Whistler Olympic Park"), the Whistler Celebration Plaza and the [Whistler Sliding Centre](/wiki/Whistler_Sliding_Centre "Whistler Sliding Centre").

The 2010 Winter Games marked the first time that the energy consumption of the Olympic venues was tracked in real time and made available to the public. Energy data was collected from the metering and building automation systems of nine of the Olympic venues and was displayed online through the Venue Energy Tracker project.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Media coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Media coverage")]

See also: [List of 2010 Winter Olympics broadcasters](/wiki/List_of_2010_Winter_Olympics_broadcasters "List of 2010 Winter Olympics broadcasters")

The Olympic Games in Vancouver were broadcast worldwide by a number of television broadcasters. As rights for the 2010 Games have been packaged with those for the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), broadcasters were largely identical for both events.

The host broadcaster was Olympic Broadcasting Services Vancouver, a subsidiary of the IOC's new in-house broadcasting unit [Olympic Broadcasting Services](/wiki/Olympic_Broadcasting_Services "Olympic Broadcasting Services") (OBS). The 2010 Olympics marked the first Games where the host broadcasting facilities were provided solely by OBS.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The executive director of Olympic Broadcasting Services Vancouver was [Nancy Lee](/wiki/Nancy_Lee_(producer) "Nancy Lee (producer)"), a former producer and executive for [CBC Sports](/wiki/CBC_Sports "CBC Sports").[[31]](#cite_note-lee-31) The official broadcast theme was a piece called "City of Ice" composed by [Rob May](/wiki/Rob_May "Rob May") and [Simon Hill](/wiki/Simon_Hill "Simon Hill").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In Canada, the Games were the first Olympic Games broadcast by a new [Olympic Broadcast Media Consortium](/wiki/Canada%27s_Olympic_Broadcast_Media_Consortium "Canada's Olympic Broadcast Media Consortium") led by [CTVglobemedia](/wiki/CTVglobemedia "CTVglobemedia") and [Rogers Media](/wiki/Rogers_Media "Rogers Media"), displacing previous broadcaster [CBC Sports](/wiki/CBC_Sports "CBC Sports"). Main English-language coverage was shown on the [CTV Television Network](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network"), while supplementary programming was mainly shown on [TSN](/wiki/The_Sports_Network "The Sports Network") and [Rogers Sportsnet](/wiki/Rogers_Sportsnet "Rogers Sportsnet"). Main French-language coverage was shown on [V](/wiki/V_(TV_network) "V (TV network)") and [RDS](/wiki/R%C3%A9seau_des_sports "Réseau des sports").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[NBC Universal](/wiki/NBC_Olympic_broadcasts "NBC Olympic broadcasts") networks televised the 2010 Winter Olympics in the United States, under a contract in which it paid US$2.2 billion for the rights to the Games and the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"). Advertising sales had slowed in comparison to previous Olympics due to the [ongoing recession](/wiki/Great_Recession_in_the_United_States "Great Recession in the United States"), and NBC projected a financial loss upwards of $250 million on the Games.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36) Due to the growth of [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media"), NBC faced particular criticism for its traditional practice of [tape delaying](/wiki/Broadcast_delay "Broadcast delay") network coverage of the Olympics for the west coast—a practice that was made more egregious by the fact that these Games took place at sites within the [Pacific Time Zone](/wiki/Pacific_Time_Zone "Pacific Time Zone").[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36) In April 2010, the network reported a financial loss of $233 million on the Games.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

The [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") (AP) announced that it would send 120 reporters, photographers, editors and videographers to cover the Games on behalf of the country's [news media](/wiki/News_media "News media").[[38]](#cite_note-eandp10-38) The cost of their Olympics coverage prompted AP to make a "real departure for the [wire service](/wiki/Wire_service "Wire service")'s online coverage". Rather than simply providing content, it partnered with more than 900 newspapers and broadcasters who split the ad revenue generated from an AP-produced multi-media package of video, photos, statistics, stories and a daily Webcast.[[38]](#cite_note-eandp10-38) AP's coverage included a [microsite](/wiki/Microsite "Microsite") with [web widgets](/wiki/Web_widget "Web widget") facilitating integration with [social networking](/wiki/Social_networking "Social networking") and [bookmarking services](/wiki/Social_bookmarking "Social bookmarking").[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In France, the Games were covered by [France Télévisions](/wiki/France_T%C3%A9l%C3%A9visions "France Télévisions"), which included continuous live coverage on its website.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Torch relay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Torch relay")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Vancouver-Olympics-clock.jpg/250px-Vancouver-Olympics-clock.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vancouver-Olympics-clock.jpg)

The clock counting down to the opening of the Olympic Games in [Downtown Vancouver](/wiki/Downtown_Vancouver "Downtown Vancouver")

Main article: [2010 Winter Olympics torch relay](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics_torch_relay "2010 Winter Olympics torch relay")

The Olympic Torch Relay is the transfer of the Olympic flame from Ancient [Olympia, Greece](/wiki/Olympia,_Greece "Olympia, Greece") — where the first Olympic Games were held thousands of years ago — to the stadium of the city hosting the current Olympic Games. The flame arrives just in time for the [Opening Ceremony](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics_opening_ceremony "2010 Winter Olympics opening ceremony").

For the Vancouver 2010 Olympic Winter Games, the flame was lit in Olympia on October 22, 2009.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It then traveled from [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), over the [North Pole](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") to Canada's High Arctic and on to the West Coast and Vancouver. The relay started its long Canada journey from the British Columbia capital of [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_British_Columbia "Victoria, British Columbia"). In Canada, the torch traveled approximately 45,000 kilometres (28,000 mi) over 106 days, making it the longest relay route within one country in Olympic history. The Olympic Torch was carried by approximately 12,000 Canadians and reached over 1,000 communities.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Celebrity torchbearers included [Arnold Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger"),[[43]](#cite_note-emotions-43) [Steve Nash](/wiki/Steve_Nash "Steve Nash"),[[44]](#cite_note-44) [Matt Lauer](/wiki/Matt_Lauer "Matt Lauer"),[[45]](#cite_note-45) [Justin Morneau](/wiki/Justin_Morneau "Justin Morneau"),[[46]](#cite_note-46) [Michael Bublé](/wiki/Michael_Bubl%C3%A9 "Michael Bublé"),[[47]](#cite_note-47) [Bob Costas](/wiki/Bob_Costas "Bob Costas"),[[48]](#cite_note-48) [Shania Twain](/wiki/Shania_Twain "Shania Twain"),[[49]](#cite_note-49) and hockey greats including [Sidney Crosby](/wiki/Sidney_Crosby "Sidney Crosby"),[[50]](#cite_note-50) [Wayne Gretzky](/wiki/Wayne_Gretzky "Wayne Gretzky"),[[51]](#cite_note-51) and the captains of the two [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks") teams that went to the [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/Stanley_Cup_Final "Stanley Cup Final"): [Trevor Linden](/wiki/Trevor_Linden "Trevor Linden") ([1994](/wiki/1994_Stanley_Cup_Final "1994 Stanley Cup Final"))[[52]](#cite_note-52) and [Stan Smyl](/wiki/Stan_Smyl "Stan Smyl") ([1982](/wiki/1982_Stanley_Cup_Final "1982 Stanley Cup Final")).[[53]](#cite_note-53)

## The Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The Games")]

### Participating National Olympic Committees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Participating National Olympic Committees")]

82 [National Olympic Committees](/wiki/National_Olympic_Committees "National Olympic Committees") (NOC) entered teams in the 2010 Winter Olympics.[[54]](#cite_note-WinterGames-54) Cayman Islands, Colombia, Ghana, Montenegro, Pakistan, Peru and Serbia made their winter Olympic debuts. Morocco returned to the Winter games after an 18-year absence, and Jamaica and Mexico returned after 8 years. Seven countries, Costa Rica, Kenya, Luxembourg, Madagascar, Thailand, Venezuela and the Virgin Islands which were at the 2006 Games, did not participate in 2010.

Tonga sought to make its Winter Olympic debut by entering a single competitor in luge, attracting some media attention, but he crashed in the final round of qualifying.[[55]](#cite_note-55)
Luxembourg qualified two athletes[[56]](#cite_note-fis-ski.com-56) but did not participate because one did not reach the criteria set by the NOC[[57]](#cite_note-57) and the other was injured[[58]](#cite_note-58) before the Games. The Philippines also sought to qualify three athletes, one in figure skating pairs and a single one in snowboarding, but failed to make the qualifying standards.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Below is a map of the participating nations and a list of the nations with the number of competitors indicated in brackets.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

The opening and closing ceremonies and the events categorized as ice sports (excluding bobsleigh, luge and skeleton) were held in Vancouver and Richmond. The sports categorized as "Nordic events" were held in the Callaghan Valley located just to the west of Whistler. All alpine skiing events were held on [Whistler Mountain](/wiki/Whistler_Blackcomb "Whistler Blackcomb") (Creekside) and sliding events (bobsleigh, luge and skeleton) were held on [Blackcomb Mountain](/wiki/Whistler_Blackcomb "Whistler Blackcomb"). [Cypress Mountain](/wiki/Cypress_Mountain "Cypress Mountain") (located in [Cypress Provincial Park](/wiki/Cypress_Provincial_Park "Cypress Provincial Park") in [West Vancouver](/wiki/West_Vancouver "West Vancouver")) hosted the freestyle skiing (aerials, moguls and ski cross), and all snowboard events (half-pipe, parallel giant slalom, snowboard cross).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/SidneyCrosby2010WinterOlympicsgold.jpg/250px-SidneyCrosby2010WinterOlympicsgold.jpg)](/wiki/File:SidneyCrosby2010WinterOlympicsgold.jpg)

[Sidney Crosby](/wiki/Sidney_Crosby "Sidney Crosby") celebrates moments after scoring the gold medal-winning goal over [the United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States men's national ice hockey team").

Vancouver 2010 was also the first winter Olympics in which both men's and women's hockey were played on a narrower, [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")-sized ice rink,[[61]](#cite_note-VANOC_shrinks_ice-61) measuring 200 ft × 85 ft (61 m × 26 m), instead of the international size of 200 ft × 98.5 ft (61.0 m × 30.0 m). The games were played at [General Motors Place](/wiki/General_Motors_Place "General Motors Place") (now Rogers Arena), home of the NHL's [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks"), which was temporarily renamed Canada Hockey Place for the duration of the Olympics. Utilizing this existing venue instead of building a new international-sized ice rink facility saved C$10 million in construction costs and allowed an additional 35,000 spectators to attend Olympic hockey games.[[61]](#cite_note-VANOC_shrinks_ice-61) However, some European countries expressed concern over this decision, worried that it might give North American players an advantage since they grew up playing on the smaller NHL-sized rinks.[[62]](#cite_note-62) By contrast, the only other NHL venue to host Olympic hockey, the [Calgary Flames](/wiki/Calgary_Flames "Calgary Flames")' [Olympic Saddledome](/wiki/Olympic_Saddledome "Olympic Saddledome"), started construction before Calgary won the bid for the 1988 Winter Olympics and it was designed to accommodate an international ice rink.

There were a number of events that were proposed to be included in the 2010 Winter Olympics.[[63]](#cite_note-63) On November 28, 2006, the IOC Executive Board at their meeting in [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait") voted to include [ski cross](/wiki/Ski_cross "Ski cross") in the official program.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The Vancouver Olympic Committee (VANOC) subsequently approved the event to officially be part of the Games program.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Events proposed for inclusion but ultimately rejected included:[[66]](#cite_note-66)

* [Biathlon](/wiki/Biathlon "Biathlon") mixed relay
* Mixed doubles [curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling")
* Team [alpine skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing "Alpine skiing")
* Team [bobsled](/wiki/Bobsled "Bobsled") and [skeleton](/wiki/Skeleton_(sport) "Skeleton (sport)")
* Team [luge](/wiki/Luge "Luge")
* Women's [ski jumping](/wiki/Ski_jumping "Ski jumping")

The issue over women's ski jumping being excluded ended up in the [Supreme Court of British Columbia](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_British_Columbia "Supreme Court of British Columbia") in Vancouver during April 21–24, 2009, with a verdict on July 10 excluding women's ski jumping from the 2010 Games.
[[67]](#cite_note-wskijmp-67) A request to appeal that verdict to the [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada") was subsequently denied on December 22 – a decision that marked the end of any hopes that the event would be held during Vancouver 2010.[[68]](#cite_note-wskijmpappeal-68) To alleviate the exclusion, VANOC organizers invited women from all over Canada to participate at Whistler Olympic Park, including Continental Cup in January 2009.[[67]](#cite_note-wskijmp-67) Women's ski jumping was included in the [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics") in [Sochi](/wiki/Sochi "Sochi"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia").

## Concerns and controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Concerns and controversies")]

Main article: [Concerns and controversies over the 2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Concerns_and_controversies_over_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Concerns and controversies over the 2010 Winter Olympics")

Some in the foreign press, including London 2012 organizers, had criticized the lack of city decorations to acknowledge that Vancouver was the host of the Games. The original plans to decorate the city in Olympic colours, a tradition followed by other recent Games host cities, were canceled for several reasons. Anti-Games activists repeatedly vandalized the existing 2010 Olympics monuments such as the countdown clock, forcing the city to install CCTV cameras, and adding more Games decorations would have inevitably required more security presence to deter protesters, so VANOC opted to minimize these symbols to avoid making the city a police state. Besides local opposition, budget cutbacks also forced the organizers to scale back on the elaborate plans.[[70]](#cite_note-vancitybuzz1-70)

VANOC initially benefited from an economic boom and was able to secure lucrative and record domestic sponsorships, but this boom also resulted in rapidly rising construction and labour costs. Due to these factors, as well as the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), VANOC built minimalistic functional venues with little aesthetic appeal though they were well-designed for post-Games usage. This approach, as well as the fact that most of the infrastructure already existed, meant that the direct costs of the Vancouver Games were much lower than recent Olympic games.[[70]](#cite_note-vancitybuzz1-70)

Before the Games began and as they commenced, a number of concerns and controversies surfaced and received media attention. Hours before the opening ceremony, Georgian [luger](/wiki/Luge "Luge") [Nodar Kumaritashvili](/wiki/Nodar_Kumaritashvili "Nodar Kumaritashvili") was killed during a training run after being thrown out from his luge, intensifying questions about the safety of the course and prompting[[71]](#cite_note-71) organizers to implement quick modifications. Immediately after the accident, officials attributed it to an athlete error rather than a track deficiency.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The [International Luge Federation](/wiki/International_Luge_Federation "International Luge Federation") called an emergency meeting after the accident, and all other training runs were cancelled for the day.[[73]](#cite_note-bbc-crash-73) The President of Georgia, [Mikheil Saakashvili](/wiki/Mikheil_Saakashvili "Mikheil Saakashvili"), thanked the hosts for the way that they handled Kumaritashvili's death which included a moment of silence, bringing his remains back to Georgia and a tribute to him at the opening ceremony.[[74]](#cite_note-telegraph1-74)

One critic[[75]](#cite_note-75)
questioned the choice of [Cypress Mountain](/wiki/Cypress_Mountain "Cypress Mountain") as a venue because of its potential lack of snow due to the 2009-10 [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño"). Because of this possibility, organizers had a contingency plan to truck in snow from [Manning Park](/wiki/Manning_Park "Manning Park"), about 250 kilometres (160 mi) to the east of the city. This allowed events to proceed as planned.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Political decisions involving cancellation of promised low-income housing and the creation of a community of mixed economic backgrounds for post-Games use of the [athletes' village](/wiki/2010_Olympic_Village "2010 Olympic Village") was criticized.[[77]](#cite_note-Mackin-77)

Opening ceremonies were stalled while organizers dealt with mechanical problems during the cauldron lighting ceremony.[[78]](#cite_note-mishaps-78) Speed skating events were delayed due to breakdowns of the [ice resurfacers](/wiki/Ice_resurfacer "Ice resurfacer") supplied by Olympia, an official sponsor of the Games.[[79]](#cite_note-ice_resurfacing-79)

Thousands of tickets were voided by organizers when weather conditions made standing-room-only areas unsafe.[[80]](#cite_note-spiegelol-80) Visitors were also upset that, as in past Olympics, medal ceremonies required separate admission[[80]](#cite_note-spiegelol-80) and blocks of VIP tickets reserved for sponsors and dignitaries were unused at events.[[81]](#cite_note-81) Other glitches and complaints have included confusion by officials at the start of the February 16 [men's](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_pursuit "Biathlon at the 2010 Winter Olympics – Men's pursuit") and [women's biathlon pursuit](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_pursuit "Biathlon at the 2010 Winter Olympics – Women's pursuit") races, and restricted access to the Olympic flame cauldron on the Vancouver waterfront.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)

### Opposition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Opposition")]

Opposition to the Olympic Games was expressed by activists and politicians, including [Lower Mainland](/wiki/Lower_Mainland "Lower Mainland") [mayors](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") [Derek Corrigan](/wiki/Derek_Corrigan "Derek Corrigan")[[84]](#cite_note-84)
and [Richard Walton](/wiki/North_Vancouver,_British_Columbia_(district_municipality) "North Vancouver, British Columbia (district municipality)").[[85]](#cite_note-85) Many of the public pre-Olympic events held in Vancouver were attended by [protesters](/wiki/Protest "Protest").[[86]](#cite_note-86)

On Saturday, February 13, as part of a week-long Anti-Olympic Convergence, protesters smashed windows of the [Downtown Vancouver](/wiki/Downtown_Vancouver "Downtown Vancouver") location of [The Hudson's Bay](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_(retailer) "Hudson's Bay (retailer)") [department store](/wiki/Department_store "Department store").[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) Protesters later argued that the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company"), "has been a symbol of [colonial oppression](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") for centuries" as well as a major sponsor of the 2010 Olympics.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

Some of the issues reflected in the opposition continue the themes identified in opposition to all Olympic games, some of which are outlined in anti-Olympics activist and Professor of Sociology Helen Jefferson Lenskyj's books *Olympic Industry Resistance* (2007) and *Inside the Olympic Industry* (2000), which examined a number of different Olympic Games prior to the 2010 Winter Olympics.[[90]](#cite_note-90)
These issues of concern, underlying the opposition to any and all Olympic Games, include:

* Displacement of [low-income](/wiki/Low-income "Low-income") residents.[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)
* Anticipated [human trafficking](/wiki/Human_trafficking "Human trafficking") for the purpose of forced prostitution.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

#### First Nations opposition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: First Nations opposition")]

Although the Aboriginal governments of the [Squamish](/wiki/Squamish_Nation "Squamish Nation"), [Musqueam](/wiki/Musqueam_Indian_Band "Musqueam Indian Band"), [Lil'wat](/wiki/Lil%27wat_First_Nation "Lil'wat First Nation") and [Tsleil-Waututh](/wiki/Tsleil-Waututh_First_Nation "Tsleil-Waututh First Nation") (the "Four Host First Nations"), on whose traditional territory the Games were held, signed a protocol in 2004[[98]](#cite_note-98) in support of the games,[[99]](#cite_note-99) there was opposition to the Olympics from some [indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_people "Indigenous people") groups and supporters. Although the [Lil'wat](/wiki/Lil%27wat "Lil'wat") branch of the [St'at'imc Nation](/wiki/St%27at%27imc_Nation "St'at'imc Nation") is a co-host of the Games, a splinter group from the [Seton](/wiki/Seton_Lake_First_Nation "Seton Lake First Nation") band known as the St'at'imc of Sutikalh, who have also opposed the Cayoosh Ski Resort, feared the Olympics would bring unwanted tourism and real estate sales to their territory.[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Local aboriginal people, as well as Canadian [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"), initially expressed concern over the choice of an [inukshuk](/wiki/Inukshuk "Inukshuk") as the symbol of the Games, with some Inuit leaders such as former [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut") Commissioner [Peter Irniq](/wiki/Peter_Irniq "Peter Irniq") stating that the inukshuk is a culturally important symbol to them. He said that the "Inuit never build inuksuit with head, legs and arms. I have seen inuksuit [built] more recently, 100 years maybe by non-Inuit in Nunavut, with head, legs and arms. These are not called inuksuit. These are called [inunguat](/wiki/Inunguat "Inunguat"), imitation of man."[[102]](#cite_note-102) Local aboriginal groups also expressed annoyance that the design did not reflect the [Coast Salish](/wiki/Coast_Salish_peoples "Coast Salish peoples") and [Interior Salish](/wiki/Interior_Salish "Interior Salish") native culture from the region the Games are being held in, but rather that of the Inuit, who are indigenous to the Arctic far from Vancouver.

### Doping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Doping")]

On March 11, 2010, it was reported that the Polish cross country skier [Kornelia Marek](/wiki/Kornelia_Marek "Kornelia Marek") was tested positive for [EPO](/wiki/Erythropoietin "Erythropoietin") by the [Polish Olympic Committee](/wiki/Polish_Olympic_Committee "Polish Olympic Committee"). If found guilty of doping by the International Olympic Committee, Marek and the relay teams would be disqualified and stripped of their Vancouver results. She would also be banned from the [next Winter Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics") in [Sochi](/wiki/Sochi "Sochi"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), in 2014.

Marek denied taking any banned substances, but the backup "B" sample from the Vancouver doping lab confirmed the "A" sample.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

On October 9, 2017, the IOC announced that three positive doping cases had been found from their re-analysis programme from the 2010 games. All three cases belonged to the same athlete, whose identity was not released at the time. The IOC had re-tested 1,195 urine samples from the games out of the 1,710 taken, which equates to 70%, as part of their re-analysis programme.[[104]](#cite_note-104)
Weeks later, the athlete was confirmed to be Slovenian biathlete [Teja Gregorin](/wiki/Teja_Gregorin "Teja Gregorin").[[105]](#cite_note-105)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Prime_Minister_of_Canada_Stephen_Harper_%26_Governor_General_of_Canada_Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean_%40_2010_Vancouver_Olympic_Winter_Games_Heads_of_State_Reception.jpg/250px-Prime_Minister_of_Canada_Stephen_Harper_%26_Governor_General_of_Canada_Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean_%40_2010_Vancouver_Olympic_Winter_Games_Heads_of_State_Reception.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister_of_Canada_Stephen_Harper_%26_Governor_General_of_Canada_Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean_@_2010_Vancouver_Olympic_Winter_Games_Heads_of_State_Reception.jpg)

[Prime Minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") [Stephen Harper](/wiki/Stephen_Harper "Stephen Harper") and [Governor General of Canada](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") [Michaelle Jean](/wiki/Michaelle_Jean "Michaelle Jean") at the 2010 Vancouver Winter Olympic Games Heads of State Reception

The massive celebratory crowds in downtown Vancouver were highly praised by the IOC. [Jacques Rogge](/wiki/Jacques_Rogge "Jacques Rogge"), the president of IOC, indicated that "the way Vancouver embraced these Games was extraordinary. This is really something unique and has given a great atmosphere for these Games."[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107) The atmosphere surrounding the Olympics, and its inclusion of foreign delegates and guests, was also praised, with many seasoned Winter Olympic observers putting the games at, or near, the top of the list of best ever Winter Olympics.[[108]](#cite_note-108) They were also the best watched Winter Olympics since the [1994 Olympics](/wiki/1994_Olympics "1994 Olympics") in Lillehammer.[[109]](#cite_note-109) They are also mentioned alongside the [Sydney 2000](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics") Summer games in regards to the best atmosphere. A large part is credited to the citizens of Vancouver, British Columbia and Canada.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

Some members of the media (mostly, and particularly the [British media](/wiki/Media_of_the_United_Kingdom "Media of the United Kingdom")) criticized the Own the Podium and criticized the celebrations as having been somewhat nationalistic,[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112) but this was not an opinion shared by many. Some suggested that the British media were making these criticisms in order to make the upcoming [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") more appealing.[[113]](#cite_note-113) Lord [Sebastian Coe](/wiki/Sebastian_Coe "Sebastian Coe"), chairman of the [2012 London Olympic Games Organizing Committee](/wiki/London_Organising_Committee_of_the_Olympic_and_Paralympic_Games "London Organising Committee of the Olympic and Paralympic Games"), attended the Vancouver Olympics to see how the city coped with the challenges of hosting. Lord Coe noted the Games had "gradually recovered from its tumultuous start" and queried that he "never thought the British would find rivals in their preoccupation with the weather which is almost elevated to an Olympic event" as he credited [VANOC](/wiki/Vancouver_Organizing_Committee_for_the_2010_Olympic_and_Paralympic_Winter_Games "Vancouver Organizing Committee for the 2010 Olympic and Paralympic Winter Games") for meeting unforeseen challenges such as the unseasonably warm weather of Cypress Mountain. Coe added "Rarely have I seen a host city so passionate and so ready to embrace the Games".[[74]](#cite_note-telegraph1-74)[[114]](#cite_note-114)

The Vancouver Olympics also organized and hosted the first [Pride House](/wiki/Pride_House "Pride House") for [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") athletes in the history of the event.[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117)

### Funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Funding")]

Directly as a result of Canada's medal performance at the 2010 Olympics, the [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") announced in the [2010 federal budget](/wiki/2010_Canadian_federal_budget "2010 Canadian federal budget"), a new commitment of $34 million over the next two years towards programs for athletes planning to compete in future Olympics.[[118]](#cite_note-118) This is in addition to the $11 million per year federal government commitment to the [Own the Podium](/wiki/Own_the_Podium "Own the Podium") program.

Also, as a result of hosting the 2010 Olympics, the [British Columbia government](/wiki/British_Columbia_government "British Columbia government") pledged to restore funding for sports programs and athlete development to 2008 levels, which amounts to $30 million over three years.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

### Usage of venues after the Olympic Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Usage of venues after the Olympic Games")]

Unlike at previous Games, all new venues were designed with their post-Games use in mind. The [Richmond Olympic Oval](/wiki/Richmond_Olympic_Oval "Richmond Olympic Oval") was converted from a speed skating oval into an indoor multi-sports centre,[[120]](#cite_note-120) the [Hillcrest Centre](/wiki/Hillcrest_Centre "Hillcrest Centre") is now a multi-purpose [community centre](/wiki/Community_centre "Community centre") with hockey rink and swimming pool[[121]](#cite_note-121) and [Whistler Olympic Park](/wiki/Whistler_Olympic_Park "Whistler Olympic Park") is used for public recreation, athlete training, and competition events.[[122]](#cite_note-122)
